title: How to Choose the Right WAEC Subject Combination for Your Dream Career
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are in SS1 or SS2, and you are being asked to choose your WAEC subjects. The decision feels overwhelming because it is connected to everything that comes after. Your subject combination determines which university courses you qualify for. Your university course determines your career options. A wrong choice now can close doors that are difficult to reopen later. The good news is that the WAEC subject requirements for most careers are well-established and publicly available. Here is how to match your subjects to your career goals so that you keep as many doors open as possible.

How Subject Combinations Work

WAEC requires all students to take a set of core subjects: English Language, Mathematics, one Nigerian Language (or another approved language), and usually Civic Education. Beyond these core subjects, you choose additional subjects from three broad categories: Sciences, Arts and Humanities, and Commercial or Social Sciences. The specific combination you choose determines whether you are classified as a Science, Arts, or Commercial student, and that classification determines your eligibility for different university courses.

Most students take between eight and nine subjects in total. The core subjects account for four or five of these. The remaining four or five are your elective subjects, and these are where your career alignment happens. A student aiming for Medicine must take Biology, Chemistry, and Physics as electives. A student aiming for Law must take Literature in English and Government. A student aiming for Accounting must take Economics and Commerce. The core subjects get you the certificate. The elective subjects get you into your chosen course.

The most common mistake students make is choosing electives based on what their friends are taking or what they think will be easy. This is shortsighted. Your electives should be chosen based on the published admission requirements for the university course you intend to pursue. The JAMB brochure, updated annually, lists the required WAEC subjects for every course at every Nigerian university. If you have not consulted it, you are making one of the most important decisions of your education based on guesswork.

Subject Combinations for Popular Careers

Medicine and Surgery: English, Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry, Physics. These five are non-negotiable. Some universities also require a credit in English and Mathematics, so do not neglect these core subjects while focusing on the sciences. The competition for Medicine is intense. Aim for at least B3 in Biology, Chemistry, and Physics. A C6 may meet the minimum requirement but will not make you competitive at top universities.

Engineering: English, Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, and one other science or technical subject such as Further Mathematics or Technical Drawing. Mathematics and Physics are the most important. A credit in Further Mathematics is not always required but is highly recommended, especially for competitive programs. Engineering courses are mathematically demanding, and a strong foundation in Further Mathematics makes the transition to university-level engineering mathematics significantly easier.

Law: English, Mathematics, Literature in English, Government, and one other Arts or Social Science subject such as Civic Education, History, or Christian or Islamic Religious Studies. Literature in English is the most important elective for Law. It trains you to read closely, analyze texts, and construct arguments, all skills that are central to legal education. A student who dislikes reading and writing should reconsider Law, regardless of their WAEC subject combination.

Accounting and Business Administration: English, Mathematics, Economics, and two other Commercial subjects such as Commerce, Financial Accounting, or Government. Mathematics is especially important for Accounting. University-level accounting involves quantitative analysis, and a weak mathematics foundation will make the coursework unnecessarily difficult.

Computer Science: English, Mathematics, Physics, and two other subjects, typically Chemistry or Further Mathematics. Mathematics is the foundation. Physics supports the hardware and electronics aspects of computer science. Further Mathematics, while not always required, strengthens your application and your preparation for the mathematical rigor of a computer science degree.

Mass Communication: English, Mathematics, Literature in English, Government, and one other Arts or Social Science subject. English and Literature are the most relevant. Mass Communication involves extensive writing, and your WAEC English and Literature grades signal your readiness for that demand.

Nursing and Pharmacy: English, Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry, Physics. The requirements are identical to Medicine. The competition is slightly less intense but still significant. Aim for credit passes across all five subjects, with B grades or higher in the sciences for competitive programs.

What to Do If You Are Undecided

If you do not yet know what career you want, choose a subject combination that keeps the most doors open. The science combination, English, Mathematics, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, and one or two other subjects, is the most versatile. It qualifies you for Medicine, Engineering, Pharmacy, Computer Science, and most science-based courses. You can also switch to Commercial or Arts courses later because English and Mathematics, the universal requirements, are included.

The Commercial combination, English, Mathematics, Economics, and Commerce, is also flexible. It qualifies you for Accounting, Business Administration, Economics, and related courses. The Arts combination is the least flexible, as it locks you out of most science and commercial courses. If you are undecided and leaning toward Arts, consider keeping Mathematics as an elective even if it is not required for your intended Arts courses. It preserves the option to switch later.

Check Requirements Before You Finalize

Before you submit your subject registration, verify the requirements for at least three universities you would consider attending. University requirements are not identical. One university may require Further Mathematics for Engineering while another does not. One may accept Civic Education as a social science subject while another does not. The JAMB brochure is the authoritative source for these requirements. It is available online and updated annually. Do not rely on what your friends or even your teachers tell you. Verify against the official publication.

Also check whether your intended course requires any subject at a specific grade level. Some universities require a minimum of B3 in English for Law, or a minimum of B3 in Mathematics for Engineering. Meeting the subject requirement is not enough. You must meet the grade requirement as well. Knowing these requirements early allows you to set clear targets for your preparation.

The Bottom Line

Your WAEC subject combination is one of the most consequential decisions of your secondary education. Choose your electives based on the published admission requirements for your intended university course, not based on what your friends are taking or what seems easy. If you are undecided, choose the science combination to keep the most doors open, or the commercial combination for flexibility. Verify requirements for at least three target universities before finalizing your registration. The time you invest in choosing the right subjects now will save you from closed doors later.
